content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

December 21, 1976

County Counsel
County of Alameda
Administration Building
1221 Oak Street
Oakland, California 94612

Dear Sir or Madam:

On December 16, 1976, pursuant to 2 Cal. Admin. Code
§18751, the Fair Political Practices Commission exempted
the Bay Area Sewage Services Agency from the requirement
to develop a Conflict of Interest Code applicable to the
Agency. The Agency, pursuant to Assembly Bill 3041,
Chapter 682, will cease to exist on January 1, 1977.
The legislation provides that the Agency's successor,
the County Auditor of the County of Alameda, shall wind
up the affairs of the Agency. Because of the authority
the Auditor shall extend to a jurisdiction larger than
the County of Alameda; therefore, it will be necessary
for the County of Alameda, pursuant to a Conflict of Interest Code,
to disclose reportable interests held within the juris-
diction of the Bay Area Sewage Services Agency.

It is the view of the Commission that the extra county
jurisdiction of the Auditor shall not require the Auditor
to submit a Code to the FPPC; however, the disclosure res-
ponsibilities of the Auditor within the Code submitted to
the Board of Supervisors of Alameda County should include
the broader jurisdiction applicable to the Bay Area Sewage
Services Agency.

If you or the Board of Supervisors, as code reviewing
body for the Office of the Auditor, disagree with or have
any questions regarding the foregoing, please call me at
916/322-6444. Thank you.

Sincerely,

Delbert L. Spurlock, Jr., Chief
Conflicts of Interest Division

DLS:glb

cc: Office of the Auditor
    County of Alameda

TECHNICAL ASSISTANCE ADMINISTRATION 916-322-5660 • EXECUTIVE LEGAL 322-5901 • ENFORCEMENT CONFLICT OF INTEREST 322-6441

sections.question_synthetic: Whether the Bay Area Sewage Services Agency must develop a Conflict of Interest Code given that it will cease to exist on January 1, 1977, and its successor (the County Auditor) will have jurisdiction extending beyond Alameda County.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The Bay Area Sewage Services Agency is exempt from developing a Conflict of Interest Code due to its pending dissolution. However, the County Auditor's Conflict of Interest Code submitted to the Alameda County Board of Supervisors must include disclosure responsibilities that account for the Auditor's broader extra-county jurisdiction previously held by the Agency.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Whether the Bay Area Sewage Services Agency must develop a Conflict of Interest Code given that it will cease to exist on January 1, 1977, and its successor (the County Auditor) will have jurisdiction extending beyond Alameda County.

CONCLUSION: The Bay Area Sewage Services Agency is exempt from developing a Conflict of Interest Code due to its pending dissolution. However, the County Auditor's Conflict of Interest Code submitted to the Alameda County Board of Supervisors must include disclosure responsibilities that account for the Auditor's broader extra-county jurisdiction previously held by the Agency.
embedding.first_500_words: This letter is from the Fair Political Practices Commission of the State of California, dated December 21, 1976. It is addressed to County Counsel of the County of Alameda and discusses the exemption of the Bay Area Sewage Services Agency from the requirement to develop a Conflict of Interest Code applicable to the Agency. The letter explains that the Agency, pursuant to Assembly Bill 3041, Chapter 682, will cease to exist on January 1, 1977. The legislation provides that the Agency's successor, the County Auditor of the County of Alameda, shall wind up the affairs of the Agency. Because of the authority the Auditor shall extend to a jurisdiction larger than the County of Alameda; therefore, it will be necessary for the Auditor, pursuant to a Conflict of Interest Code, to disclose reportable interests held within the jurisdiction of the Bay Area Sewage Services Agency. The Commission states that the extra county jurisdiction of the Auditor shall not require the Auditor to submit a Code to the FPPC; however, the disclosure responsibilities of the Auditor within the Code submitted to the Board of Supervisors of Alameda County should include the broader jurisdiction applicable to the Bay Area Sewage Services Agency. If you or the Board of Supervisors, as code reviewing body for the Office of the Auditor, disagree with or have any questions regarding the foregoing, please call me at 916/322-6444. Thank you. Sincerely, Delbert L. Spurlock, Jr., Chief Conflicts of Interest Division DLS:glb cc: Office of the Auditor County of Alameda
embedding.summary: FPPC advises that the Bay Area Sewage Services Agency is exempt from developing a Conflict of Interest Code because it will cease to exist on January 1, 1977 under AB 3041. The successor County Auditor's existing Code should incorporate disclosure requirements for the broader jurisdictional interests previously held by the Agency.
